Scenario.overview: Water and Wastewater Systems Complex A sophisticated cyber attack targeting water and wastewater systems involves multiple phases, starting with reconnaissance to identify potential entry points, followed by resource development to establish command and control infrastructure. Initial access is gained through drive-by compromise of internet-facing assets. Execution occurs via command-line interfaces using Bash scripts. Persistence is maintained through scheduled tasks. Privilege escalation abuses elevation control mechanisms. Defense evasion includes disabling or modifying security tools. Credential access attempts involve brute forcing user accounts. Discovery phase maps the network configuration. Lateral movement utilizes alternate authentication materials. Collection gathers sensitive data from repositories, while exfiltration transfers data covertly over alternative protocols. Finally, impact is achieved by disrupting inter-process communication.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Network Traffic, Scheduled Task, User Account, File System, Application Log, Logon Session, DNS Record, Module
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Delivery Method, Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Response Metadata (Mechanism), Bash Script, Scheduled Task/Job, Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism, Disable or Modify Tools, User Account Authentication (Mechanism), System Network Configuration Discovery, Application Log Content (Mechanism), Logon Session Creation (Mechanism), Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol, Module Load (Mechanism)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers engage in active scanning activities to map the network nodes, hosts, devices, and routes of the water and wastewater systems.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 522 Name: Malicious Hardware Component Replacement
stage.description: The attackers acquire infrastructure such as servers to set up command and control channels for future operations.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 535 Name: Malicious Gray Market Hardware
stage.description: Attackers exploit unpatched systems via malicious websites leading to drive-by compromises.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 88 Name: OS Command Injection
stage.description: Malicious scripts or commands are executed on compromised systems to achieve further control.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 654 Name: Credential Prompt Impersonation
stage.description: Maintain access through the creation of scheduled tasks that execute malicious payloads periodically.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Gaining higher-level permissions by exploiting weaknesses in the system's elevation control mechanisms.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 578 Name: Disable Security Software
stage.description: Disabling or modifying security tools to avoid detection and maintain stealth within the network.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 49 Name: Password Brute Forcing
stage.description: Attempting to gain valid credentials by repeatedly trying different combinations of usernames and passwords.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: Understanding the network topology by examining file systems and configurations.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: Moving across the network using stolen credentials obtained through previous phases of the attack.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 277 Name: Data Interchange Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Gathering sensitive information from various repositories within the network.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 590 Name: IP Address Blocking
stage.description: Transferring data out of the network covertly using alternative protocols like DNS.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 276 Name: Inter-component Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Disrupting critical system functions by manipulating inter-process communications.

Scenario.summary: The Outer Space campaign targets water and wastewater systems with a complex multi-stage attack plan. Starting with reconnaissance, attackers identify entry points and develop resources for command and control. They gain initial access through drive-by compromise, execute malicious scripts, and maintain persistence via scheduled tasks. Privilege escalation and defense evasion techniques are employed to deepen infiltration. Credentials are accessed through brute force, and lateral movement is facilitated using stolen identities. Sensitive data is collected and exfiltrated covertly, culminating in impactful disruptions to system functionality.
